observations.recommendation:
Municipal Accountant
To record the remaining items of the relief goods as Inventory as stated below:



To record purchases under the Inventory account on the basis of the Acceptance and Inspection Report (AIR) and other supporting documents such as Purchase Orders (POs), Delivery Receipts a/or Supplier’s Invoice.

To record expenses on the basis of the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) together with the supporting RIS.

 Municipal Treasurer as the General Services Officer

To consolidate the RIS in the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the Office of the Municipal Accountant for proper recording of its issuances in order to reflect the correct Inventory and Expense balances.
Requisitioning Office/Departments
To refrain from attaching RIS in the disbursement vouchers as one of the supporting documents for such shall only be used upon issuance of a particular item carried in stocks.

The Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:

Conduct thorough review, with the involvement of other concerned personnel, and analyze the details of receivables to ascertain their correctness, validity and collectability and send demand letters to persons who have known address to facilitate collections; and

File a request for write-off to the Commission on Audit the account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected which should be duly supported with certified relevant documents pertaining to the accounts pursuant with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

The Municipal Accountant to:

Prepare demand letters for the liquidation of the said cash advances from accountable officers and monitor the timely submission of liquidation report within the reglementary in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

Suspend the salary of the accountable officers who are still employed in the municipality who failed to liquidate their cash advances aging from one (1) year and over as of grant date.

The LCE to file request for write-off of dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated CAs and fund transfers pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

The  Municipal Treasurer execute a yearly contract of lease embodied in a public instrument for every market stall owners/occupants and follow the standards/guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 88-282A to ensure that interests of both parties are protected, ambiguities and/or disagreements s are avoided, and peaceful operation of the municipal public market is sustained.
	
Further, the Municipal Treasurer through its assigned collectors in the market stall to exert more efforts in collecting delinquent accounts amounting to  P49,800.00 as of December 31, 2020 and as well as prepare and maintain individual records of the market stall lessees for easy monitoring of payments and imposition of charges for non-payment thereof.

The Management re-examine and update the Local Revenue Code to revise the existing ordinances in accordance with the Local Government Code and require the Local Finance Committee to consider on what provisions of the Revenue Code that needs to be retained, deleted and added to have sound projections of the LGUs financial requirements. Further, updating and rationalizing the tax rates of the Revenue Code once every five years as required under Section 191 of the Local Government Code helps to ensure the sustainability and resilience of various local taxes.

The management establish and maintain a GAD database containing sex-disaggregated data and gender statistics in accordance with Item 4 (B) of the above-cited JMC to ensure that PPAs included in the GPB truly give priority that will address emerging and/or continuing issues and concerns such as provision of basic services and facilities that protect and fulfill women’s human rights, pursue women’s empowerment and gender equality.

We recommended that the municipal Inventory Committee conduct the required physical count of all inventory accounts in order to establish the existence and determine the reliability of the account balances shown in the financial statements at year-end. Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant maintain the required stock cards and supplies ledger cards for each type of supplies, respectively, pursuant to Sections 119 and 120 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units,
observations.reason: The Management did not conduct physical count of its Inventory accounts. The department responsible for the physical count were not aware that they should conduct a physical verification annually.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the DSWD personnel submit to the Municipal Accountant the required documents in accordance with Section 8 of Joint Memorandum Circular No.1, Series of 2020, and the latter to attach the required documents to the payrolls in order to ensure the validity, propriety and legality thereof, otherwise the same shall not be passed in audit.
observations.managementAction: We will try to retrieve back the documents re: SAC or the Social Amelioration Card of the recipients mentioned in the AOM.
observations.reason: When the payroll was made for ESP or the SAC or the Social Emergency Subsidy Program, the basis was the recipients mentioned in the AOM but due to the lack of proper filing inside the office of MSWD and also due to the change in personnel, some documents were lost.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management: a) Instruct the Local Finance Committee to process the immediate return of the unutilized PDAF funds to the National Treasury; b) Submit a written explanation why the said funds have not been returned despite the SC ruling declaring it unconstitutional and why disbursements were still made after such declaration; c) Submit a summary of disbursements from November 19, 2013 to December 31, 2014 with details which lead to the remaining recorded balance of ₱72,846,900.30 as at December 31, 2014.
observations.managementAction: Management insisted on its legality and did not return the PDAF to national treasury.

observations.recommendation: It is recommended that Management record purchases of supplies to the respective inventory accounts, require the use of the RIS for issuance of supplies, prepare a weekly SSMI to be used as basis for recognition of the expense, as well as maintain perpetual inventory records to be reconciled periodically with the Municipal Treasure’s records in order to fairly present expenses and inventories in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Due to the current system implemented, tracking of inventory items and utilization is difficult, thus Management still opted to implement the warehousing system later.
observations.reason: Management opted not to implement immediately due to current system constraints.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:
1.12 We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Accountant to record the adjustment to Government Equity account due to understatement of expense as stated below:
Prior Period Adjustments 			 1,399,479.05
Drugs and Medicines for Distribution 		    1,399,479.05
#
Government Equity 			 1,399,479.05
Prior Period Adjustments 			      	1,399,479.05

1.13 We further recommended to the Municipal Accountant to record expenses on the basis of the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) together with the supporting RIS.

Recommendations:
2.4 We recommended that Management:
Establish an Inventory Committee dedicated only to conduct physical count of Local Road Network;
Before conducting the physical count, the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer reconcile their records to ensure that the road network system recorded in the books is reflected in the Inventory of Road Network that shall be maintained by the Municipal Engineering’s Office;
Thereafter, direct the Inventory Committee to start with the road network system recorded in the books. In the conduct of physical count, determine each component of the local roads, its costs or fair market value (if without identified costs); and
Direct the Inventory Committee to prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network.
2.5 Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant –
Prepare and maintain the Local Road Network Ledger Card (LRNLC) separately from each of the road component namely: a) road lot; b) road pavement; c) drainage and slope protection structures; and d) other miscellaneous structures;
Request records of the municipal road lots from the Municipal Engineering’s Office and Municipal Assessor’s Office and record the same at its fair market value; and
Complete the disclosure of the Road Network System in the Notes to Financial Statements by providing the total cost of the road networks.
2.6 Furthermore, it is recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer/MGSO, to prepare, maintain and regularly update the Local Road Network Property Card (LRNPC), in accordance with the prescribed format per COA Circular No. 2015-008.
2.7 Lastly, we recommended that Management direct the Municipal Assessor and Municipal Engineer to reconcile their records to determine the actual municipal roads, assess the same, and forward the information to the Municipal Accountant for the recording in the books.

Recommendation:
3.8 We recommended that Management to:
a. Consider requiring all department heads (i.e., Municipal Health Office and DSWD Office) accountable for the inventories, to conduct physical count of inventories on a semestral basis using the RPCI and submit the same to the MTO for validation and confirmation of the reported stocks on hand.
b. Require all end-users/department heads to maintain stock cards for all stocks including donated goods and regularly update, monitor, balance and reconcile the stock cards against stocks on hand;
c. Require all end-users/departments and MTO to prepare and timely submit to Accounting Office the RIS and RSMI for all inventories issued including donated goods issued during the year.
d. Require Municipal Accountant to effect necessary adjustments to correct inventory balances under trust fund as suggested below:
Prior Period Adjustments 					2,886,360.46
Drugs and Medicines for Distribution 			2,686,360.71
Other Supplies and Materials					  	  199,999.75
#
Government Equity 						2,886,360.46
Prior Period Adjustments					2,886,360.46
#

Recommendation:
4.8 We recommended the LCE to:
Mandate the Inventory Committee for the preparation of Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) containing the specific duties and assignment of its members from start up to its targeted final completion date to be able to come up with the required report on the physical inventory;
Relieve the personnel who were designated as Inventory Committee members of their present and regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is finally completed and submitted their output/reports following the specific guidelines and procedures for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances of the LGU.

Recommendations:
5.9 We recommended the Management to direct the Municipal Treasurer to cause the preparation of the Inventory and Inspection Report for Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) by the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the disposal of unserviceable properties.
5.10 We further recommended the Management to direct the inventory Committee to expedite the disposal of unserviceable properties following the procedures set forth in COA Circular No. 92-386 to prevent further deterioration and to realize income therefrom if found to be saleable; and
5.11 Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant to make necessary adjustments in the books once disposal records have been prepared and verified.

Recommendations:
6.8 We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to request for write-off for dormant receivable accounts and unliquidated cash advances which settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained pursuant to COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
6.9 Likewise, we further recommended to the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare demand letters for the liquidation of the said unliquidated cash advances from accountable officers and monitor the timely submission of corresponding liquidation report/s in accordance with COA Circular No. 97-002.

Recommendations:
7.7 We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to:
1. Conduct thorough review, with the involvement of other concerned personnel, and analyze the details of receivables to ascertain their correctness, validity and collectability and send demand letters to persons who have known address to facilitate collections; and
2. File a request for write-off to the Commission on Audit the account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected which should be duly supported with certified relevant documents pertaining to the accounts pursuant with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Recommendations:
9.7 We reiterated our previous recommendation  that the Local Chief Executive to direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately execute a yearly contract of lease embodied in a public instrument for every market stall owners/occupants and follow the standards/guidelines provided under COA Circular No. 88-282A to ensure that interests of both parties are protected, ambiguities and/or disagreements s are avoided, and peaceful operation of the municipal public market is sustained.

Recommendations:
10.9 We recommended that the management establish and maintain a GAD database containing sex-disaggregated data and gender statistics in accordance with Item 4 (B) of the above-cited JMC to ensure that PPAs included in the GPB truly give priority that will address emerging and/or continuing issues and concerns such as the provision of basic services and facilities that protect and fulfill women’s human rights, pursue women’s empowerment and gender equality.

Recommendations:
11.6 We recommended that the LGU capacitate its GFPS on how to undertake gender analysis by sending them to training seminars. Also, require the preparation and maintenance of GAD database containing sex-disaggregated data and other gender-related information which will serve as basis in conducting gender analysis, identifying gender issues, and determining the appropriate PAPs that address the identified gender issues to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget.

Recommendations:
12.6 We reiterated our previous recommendation that Management re-examine and update the Local Revenue Code to revise the existing ordinances in accordance with the Local Government Code and require the Local Finance Committee to consider on what provisions of the Revenue Code that needs to be retained, deleted and added to have sound projections of the LGUs financial requirements. Further, updating and rationalizing the tax rates of the Revenue Code once every five years as required under Section 191 of the Local Government Code helps to ensure the sustainability and resilience of various local taxes.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) exert extra effort to secure the relevant
documents pertaining to the unrecorded withdrawals with incomplete documentary
requirements, otherwise, it shall be disallowed in audit; (b) require the Municipal
Accountant to review the reconciling items as reflected in the Bank Reconciliation
Statement and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the discrepancies noted
in accordance with COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2, 1996; and (c) verify the
closed bank accounts with abnormal balances in the books of accounts and, thereafter,
proper reconciliation shall be made to correct the deficiencies.

We further recommended that Management submit a written explanation
on the disbursement of funds with incomplete documentary requirements and
submit the status of the action(s) taken by the Management regarding the
unidentified and unreconciled differences between the balance per books and per
banks, stating the reasons therein why it remained unacted to date.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to demand the DO
to submit the paid vouchers and settle the unliquidated cash advances from CYs
2009, 2010, 2011, 2013, as mentioned in the accompanying Notes to FS, together
with an explanation of why cash advances remained unliquidated as of reporting
date.

We recommended further that Management require the Municipal
Accountant to issue final Demand Letters to the concerned personnel with unsettled
cash advances, including those who have retired from service or transferred to
other agencies.

Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provisions of COA Circular No. 97-002
dated February 10, 1997, in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advance
and Section 89 of P.D. No. 1445 in granting additional cash advance if the previous
cash advance is not settled.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to enforce the
collection of delinquent real property taxes by strictly implementing Sections 254
and 259 of R.A. No. 7160.

We further recommended that Management direct the Accounting and
Treasurer’s Offices to reconcile their records and account in tax delinquencies. We
likewise recommended that Management require the Accountant to prepare a
correcting entry to take up the understatement of ₱1,815,224.10 in RPT/SET
Receivables for CY 2021, and to henceforth ensure full compliance with the
requirement of Section 20 of the MNGAS.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
a) create an Inventory Committee that will conduct the inventory taking
full-time until its completion pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006
dated January 31, 2020 and require the periodic physical inventory of
supplies and properties as provided in Section 124, Volume I of the
NGAS Manual for LGUs.
b) direct the Municipal Accountant and Local Treasurer to maintain and
keep the necessary ledger/stock cards for all government properties and
conduct a regular reconciliation of their records as required by
regulations; and
c) require the Inventory Committee to submit to the audit team the
RPCPPE, RPCLRN and RPCI pursuant to existing rules and
regulations.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to verify its records, review and analyze its depreciation schedule taking
into consideration the provisions provided under PAG 4 and PAG 6 of IPSAS 17 to
arrive at an accurate and reliable carrying balance of the PPE, and thereafter
prepare the appropriate adjusting entries.

We further recommended that the Accounting Office transfer the completed
PPE accounts from the TF to the GF pursuant to Sections 97 and 104(i) of the
MNGAS, and maintain the corresponding Property/Equipment Ledger Cards so
that proper monitoring and accurate computation of depreciation for each PPE can
be generated.

We recommended and Management agreed to strictly adhere to the
provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 and R.A. No. 10918
and Management also agreed to submit the necessary supporting documents
relative to the procurement of drugs and medicines for review and evaluation by
the audit team.

We recommended and Management agreed to return the amount of
₱51,829.58 to the Municipality’s coffers in compliance with Section 105, paragraph
1 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 24, Chapter 2 of R.A. No. 7160, or the Local
Government Code of 1991.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Treasurer to regularly prepare a Schedule of Unreleased Checks at the end of the
year for submission to the Municipal Accountant as basis for preparing a Journal
Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the cash in bank account and recognize the liability
at every year end, and another JEV at the beginning of the ensuing year to reverse
the previous entry made. These should form part of the supporting documents to
the financial statements submitted to COA at year end.

We recommended and Management agreed to speed up the disposal process
of the unserviceable vehicles that are beyond economic repair in accordance with
Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 92-386. We further
recommended that Management provide an area wherein these vehicles are kept
secured and protected from the elements, natural and man-made, to prevent
further deterioration and loss of salvageable parts of the vehicle.

We recommended and Management agreed to strengthen the internal
control system in the handling of supplies by:
1. requiring the Municipal Treasurer/Supply Officer to:
a. maintain the necessary stock and property cards to monitor the
purchases, issuances and distributions of supplies and materials;
b. conduct the regular physical count of these items, prepare the
required report of physical count and submit the same to the office of
the auditor; and
c. provide ample storage and physical safeguards for supplies in stock
against damage and risk of loss.
2. directing the Accounting Unit to follow the perpetual inventory method
in the purchase of supplies and materials, to include the maintenance of
supply and property ledger cards in compliance with Chapter 7, Sections
114 and 121 of the MNGAS, and to make the necessary adjusting entries
to properly recognize the appropriate inventory accounts upon purchase
instead of expenses.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the concurrent
supply officer to have all the vehicles of the Municipality marked “For Official Use
Only,” including the name and the logo of the local government unit, in compliance
with COA Circular No. 75-006 dated November 7, 1975. We further recommended
that the motor vehicles bear the authorized government plate and be secured in a
garage provided by the LGU to ensure their safety.

We recommended and Management agreed to (a) furnish the Auditor copies of
the contracts, purchase orders, and certificates of acceptance, together with all
supporting documents thereto, within the required timeframe; (b) indicate all the
basic information in the purchase order as required under COA Circular Nos. 96-
010 and 2009-001 to ensure delivery of items that meet the specification.

The Municipality enhance its compliance on setting-up Real Property Tax Receivable and Special Education Tax Receivable requiring the:

Municipal Treasurer to update the RPT Account Registers/Taxpayer’s index cards and submit the corresponding duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year to the Municipal Accountant; and 

Municipal Accountant to set-up the required RPT/SET receivables at the beginning of each year based on the certified list of taxpayers provided by the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate proper reporting of account balances in the Financial Statements.

Direct the personnel concerned to provide the necessary data on the transactions presented as to the basic information of the description of the items and the quantity of goods received by the beneficiaries; and

Monitor the Property Officer/Custodian on the preparation and maintenance of necessary reports and inventory list of all procured and donated items in accordance with COA Circular No. 2014-002 dated April 15, 2014;

The members of the BAC the following:

To cause the submission of required Mayor’s Permit in compliance of the above-quoted provision.

Post all   procurement of goods and supplies related to COVID-19 relative to the Procurement Projects undertaken in the GPPB Online Portal the necessary data as enumerated in the GPPB Circular issuances. These data or information shall be used by the GPPB and its Technical Support Office to comply with the reportorial requirements under the Bayanihan Act.  

Ensure that Omnibus Sworn Statement (OSS) contains the name of the procurement undertaken and must be an original copy. This OSS is required in lieu of the performance and warranty securities required for Emergency Procurement under the Bayanihan Act.

To direct the personnel concerned to provide necessary data on the transactions presented as to minimum information such as corresponding items or quantity for every meal and other related information as mentioned in the preceding paragraphs in order to establish proper liquidation on the disbursements incurred and to present adequate information that can establish the actions taken that transpired during the distribution of food packs.

The Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to always update her cashbooks by recording all collections/disbursements daily in order to reflect/establish the accountability at any given date and ensures early detection of errors and the subsequent correction thereof.

Reconstitute the composition of the existing GFPS Execom and TWG to comply with item 4 of PCW-DILG-DBM and NEDA JMC No. 2013-01; and 

Initiate the strengthening of the GFPS though the solidification of its Gender Responsive Monitoring and Evaluation (M&E) System in the proper determination of GAD objectives and accomplishment of its projects, programs and activities.

We recommended that the Accounting Office draw a journal entry voucher accruing the interest earned from its time deposits in order that the financial statements are fairly presented and to henceforth ensure that the same is recognized in the books in preparing the annual financial reports.
observations.managementAction: Interest Income in CY 2020 had already been adjusted to reflect the accrual mentioned per JEV 2021-09-031374 dated 09/24/2021.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Treasurer should refrain from following Barangay officials to disburse government funds. Henceforth, only permanently appointed and duly designated employees shall perform the disbursing functions. We also recommended that the Disbursing Officer should desist from transferring cash or employees pursuant to Section 4.1.4, 4.1.5 and 4.1.6 of COA Circular 97-002. In addition, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer require the Barangay officials to submit an authenticated identification cards as basis of evaluating the signatures appearing the payrolls otherwise non-compliance thereto would constitute issuance of Notices of Disallowance.
observations.managementAction: Disbursement of the cash assistance will no longer be delegated to the barangay captain as recommended by COA furthermore, the LGU is already requiring identification cards in releasing assistance in cash or in kind thru a Receiving form.
observations.reason: Receiving of Assistance was implemented which requires ID presented and other things.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that in some cases, the Bids and Award Committee (BAC) indicate in the PO all the basis the information required under COA Circular No. 96-010 to ensure that items delivered meet specifications. We also recommended that the DSWD submit the list of recipients of the rice, mongo, fruits and vegetables with their signatures acknowledging receipt thereof and for the BAC to submit the eligibility documents of Ms. Montemayor, and explain why she still received payments as a SAP beneficiary of ₱6,000.00. Lastly, we recommended that the BAC submit a written explanation why the posting requirements under Section 9 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 were not complied with.
observations.managementAction: We will orient the personnel responsible for the preparation of the Disbursement Vouchers/ Purchase Order. A standard Receiving was already communicated to the Barangay Level as to the distribution of rice, mongo and others. A standard Receiving was already communicated to the receiving of the items at the Barangay level as distributed.
observations.reason: Personnel are not properly oriented on the proper preparation of the Purchase Order. Lack of orientation of the BAC Secretariat as to the requirements by the GPPB on emergency purchases during this pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: see to it that 1% warranty security for expendable and non-expendable supplies and materials are deducted from the amount claimed to ensure that manufacturing defects shall be corrected by the supplier to protect the interest of the LGU pursuant to 62.1 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184. Hence, the purpose of the Warranty Security is no longer applicable.
observations.managementAction: The 1% Security Deposit had been deducted from Capital Goods purchases but not deducted for consumable goods as most of the time, payment is made after the supplies or goods are consumed. Hence, the purpose of the Warranty Security is no longer applicable.
observations.reason: The 1% Security Deposit had been deducted from Capital Goods purchases but not deducted for consumable goods as most of the time, payment is made after the supplies or goods are consumed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office in identifying and sorting the “For Identification” accounts totaling ₱123,712,543.04 and to reclassify these to the proper asset accounts.
observations.managementAction: Management planned to hire replacement personnel so that the reclassification can be done in CY 2022.
observations.reason: 1. Lockdown due to COVID-19 pandemic; 2. Quarantine of personnel; and 3. Resignation/Retirement of personnel

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management withhold the salaries of incumbent personnel with unliquidated cash advances. In addition, we recommended that the municipality henceforth desist from granting cash advances to personnel of national government agencies as well as to strictly monitor and liquidation of cash advances by the grantees thereof.
observations.managementAction: Management had planned to request for write off since most of the cash advance balances are no longer collectible due to the names of accountable officers deceased, while some could no longer be traced.
observations.reason: three (3) of them had already died.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the personnel responsible for the unaccounted property be required to refund the amount to the government pursuant to a Notice of Disallowance to be issued by the audit team. We recommended that henceforth the assigned inspectors are required to strictly perform their assigned duties and responsibilities in the acceptance and inspection of purchases, and that the municipality clearly establish and define each personnel’s responsibility for government property including custodianship thereof. To prevent such to occur again in the future we will put some preventive measures by having Inventory System will be established in CY 2022 to ensure accuracy and accountability of items received and have assigned personnel to man the warehouse including proper issuance and receipt of items.
observations.managementAction: Before we recommend for salary deductions with the personnel concerned we will have to do the following: 1.) check with suppliers documents who signed on the actual receiver of the delivery 2.) we will check from the logbook on if there is any issuance of the said stocks. The LGU had already allocated funds for the purchase of a lot for sanitary landfill and requested DENR-EMB to inspect and assess the land for ecological/ geological assessment report. The first lot did not pass the said assessment report, thus, the LGU planned to purchase another one from another barangay. The dumpsite had already been moved farther from the cemetery and the perimeter fence will be placed in 2021.
observations.reason: To prevent such to occur again in the future we will put some preventive measures by having Inventory System will be established in CY 2022 to ensure accuracy and accountability of items received and have assigned personnel to man the warehouse including proper issuance and receipt of items.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer improve the municipality’s tax collection efficiency by issuing, before the end of the year, tax bills to constituents, prioritizing those who have large landholdings, and by conducting tax campaigns, tapping the assistance of the barangay officials in these efforts. We also recommended that the LGU issue notices of delinquency and impose remedies provided by law, where applicable.
observations.managementAction: We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Treasurer: a) Direct the Local Finance Committee to study and explore possibilities of increasing local revenues in order to minimize the municipality’s dependence on the IRA to finance its operation; b) Direct the Municipal Treasurer to verify from the records of the BIR the accuracy of the BIR the declared gross receipts of the taxpayers in order to assess the correct amount of tax.
observations.reason: No Tax Collection Campaign

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipality maximize the use of the appropriations for the LCPC in order to safeguard and protect the welfare of children in compliance to Section 15 of RA No. 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012.
observations.managementAction: Management had already planned to hire a permanent MSWDO to implement the LCPC- Programs.
observations.reason: The effort was focused on the COVID and other calamities.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management review the journals and ledgers to identify the government units and national government agencies to whom the amounts had been transferred to and to demand liquidation thereof. If these efforts prove futile, the municipality may request for the write-off of the receivables in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016. We further recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Accountant faithfully maintain subsidiary ledgers for all control accounts as required under the Manual on NGAs.
observations.managementAction: Revising the Local Revenue Code had already been discussed, awaiting finalization. The Municipal Treasurer had already required business owners to present the previous year’s Mayor's Permit as a reference for the amount of license fee due which should not be less than the previous year.
observations.reason: Documents for 2006 and prior were no longer available.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LGU officials request the Department of Agriculture, Region 7 for an extension of the implementation period. We also recommended that the Municipal Agriculture Office implement the programs immediately and undertake a concerted effort to address the obstacles encountered which hindered the implementation and completion of the project as scheduled.
observations.managementAction: Management had been waiting for the completion of Barangay Profiling as a requirement. Personnel from the Provincial DA conducting the barangay profiling were job order employees that keeps on changing from time to time. Once the barangay profiling will be completed and the regional office will approve the request for change in the specification of one item, the LGU will completely implement the said project.
observations.reason: A request for change in the specification in one of the items had been sent to the Regional Office and a requirement for the barangay re-profiling was required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Mayor advertise the vacancies in order to gather qualified candidates who can fill the positions in the LDRRMO in order that a dedicated office can focus on the municipality’s policies, direction and strategies to avoid or adequately manage the effects of calamities and in order that the municipality maximize Disaster Risk Reduction Management programs and projects and implement them as planned to ensure the availability of adequate facilities for the municipality in case of disaster/calamity.
observations.managementAction: LDRRMO had already been hired in CY 2020, together with staff on a 2015 Contract of Service type of ML employment.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant reconcile the cashbooks and ledgers at least quarterly in order that differences between the two records can be adjusted right away to ensure the accuracy and reliability of both records as well as to prevent the incurrence of cash overdrafts.
observations.managementAction: The reconciliation is currently on-going. Target completion is by end of December 2022, including the necessary adjustments.
observations.reason: Management lacked personnel to do the reconciliation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management prioritize the filing of applications for title of the nine untitled properties in order to secure the municipality’s ownership thereon. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant’s and Treasurer’s Offices maintain Real Property Cards, respectively, and reconcile these records as well as records of the Municipal Assessor regularly.
observations.managementAction: A request for the land titling was submitted to and received by the Registry of Deeds on October 24, 2018. Additional documents were requested and in process by the Municipal Assessor.
observations.reason: Follow up on the documents was hindered due to pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take the appropriate steps to register the parcels of land under the Torrens Title System, to require the accountant to record in the books the acquisition cost of these properties are the properties included in the annual inventory report of PPE to be submitted to the auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.
observations.managementAction: The LGU had already applied for the issuance of a free patent by the DENR. eNGAS sub-ledger accounts had been implemented.
observations.reason: The DENR and the Registry of Deeds refused to register the lots mentioned because they believe that the DepEd is the rightful owner of these properties.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management: a) formulate and implement proper garbage disposal procedures and maintenance of the dumpsite; b) find ways to separate the dump site from the public cemetery; c) secure the area with a suitable perimeter fence so that the garbage can be contained within its boundaries; and d) hire employees to maintain the dump site including the segregation of recyclable and non-recyclable wastes.
observations.managementAction: The LGU had already allocated funds for the purchase of a lot for sanitary landfill and requested DENR-EMB to inspect and assess the land for ecological/ geological assessment report. The first lot did not pass the said assessment report, thus, the LGU planned to purchase another one from another barangay. The dumpsite had already been moved farther from the cemetery and the perimeter fence will be placed in 2021.
observations.reason: Ecological and Geographical Assessment of DENR-EMB. The lack of MENRO Office to focus on this issue.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a) The Municipal Treasurer produce immediately the disbursement vouchers to support the credits to the Cash in Bank account totaling ₱12,685,325.41, otherwise the same shall be disallowed in audit pursuant to Section 4(6) of P.D. 1445; b) The Municipal Mayor institute reforms in the treasury procedures to ensure that all evidence of payment are adequately secured, and initiate recovery measures for failure to produce evidence of payments made; c) The Municipal Accountant furnish COA with copies of the demand letters sent to the Municipal Treasurer specifically for unsubmitted vouchers; exhaust all means to reconstruct the details/schedule of the Accounts Receivable in the Trust Fund; and prepare a Journal Entry Voucher reclassifying the said amount from Accounts Receivable to Due from Officers and Employees.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Inventory Committee give priority to the conduct of the physical count guided by the regulations prescribed under Section 490 of the GAAM, Volume I, reconcile the results thereof with the accounting records, prepare the report for submission to our office and pinpoint responsibility for missing properties; b) Municipal Treasurer, as property custodian in the absence of a General Services Officer, secure all copies of the ARE and update the filing thereof, ensuring that these are principally considered especially during the separation of accountable officers from the government service; c) Municipal Accountant invariably require that the ARE be fully complied with, in form and quantity, as an indispensable supporting documents for purchases of movable PPE; d) Management submit the Schedule of PPE additions and disposals from CYs 2014-2016, complete with data on the property tag, ARE, accountable officer, office/location of the equipment, and the actual DVs and ARE; otherwise, those that cannot be accounted for shall be accordingly disallowed; and e) Municipal Accountant attaches the formatted and detailed schedule of PPE additions and disposals to the yearly financial report of the LGU and regularly updates it to facilitate the conduct of the physical inventory and to pinpoint responsibility for government properties.
observations.managementAction: ARE had already been implemented starting CY 2019. Lapsing Schedule of PPE was already prepared and as part of the necessary accompanying schedules to the Annual Financial Reports submitted to COA starting report year 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: a.1) fast-track the preparation of the BRS by ensuring that personnel and assigned thereto give utmost priority to its completion in order to effect timely adjustments to the recorded balances and to pinpoint accountability for any irregular bank items or unauthorized bank transactions; a.2) establish the unreconciled amount at the beginning of CY 2016 and simultaneously commence work on two (2) fronts: 1- workback on CY 2015 and prior years until any reconciling item is fully accounted for; and, 2- work forward on CY 2016 up to the present without further delay, ensuring that the established reconciling item shall never increase but only decrease as work on the PY backlogs progress. b) Municipal Mayor closely monitor the regular submission of the BRS to the Auditor for verification, inexcusably for CY 2016 henceforth, and ensure immediate settlement of accountabilities for any unauthorized reconciling items so determined; and c) Management accord utmost importance to this critical and indispensable tool in order to minimize the risk of loss/misuse of depository funds and to avoid probable sanctions for gross negligence in the performance of duties.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendations that Management require the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Reports in its prescribed format for approval and submission by the Municipal Mayor to the Government Procurement Policy Board within 14 calendar days after the end of each semester.

We recommended that the Municipal Inventory Committee conduct the required
physical count of all inventory accounts in order to establish the existence and determine
the reliability of the account balances shown in the financial statements at year-end.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant
maintain the required stock cards and supplies ledger cards for each type of supplies,
respectively, pursuant to Sections 119 and 120 of the Manual on the New Government
Accounting System for Local Government Units, Volume I.

We recommended that the Accounting Office draw a journal entry voucher
accruing the interest earned from its time deposits in order that the financial
statements are fairly presented and to henceforth ensure that the same is
recognized in the books in preparing the annual financial reports.

We recommended that the DSWD personnel submit to the Municipal
Accountant the required supporting documents in accordance with Section 8 of
Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1, Series of 2020, and the latter to attach the
required documents to the payrolls in order to ensure the validity, propriety and
legality thereon, otherwise the same shall not be passed in audit.

We recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Treasurer should refrain
from allowing Barangay officials to disburse government funds. Henceforth, only
permanently appointed and duly designated employees shall perform the
disbursing functions. We also recommended that the Disbursing Officer should
desist from transferring cash advances to another accountable officer/elective
official/non-bonded officials or employees pursuant to Sections 4.1.4, 4.1.5 and
4.1.6 of COA Circular 97-002. In addition, we recommended that the Municipal
Treasurer require the Barangay officials to submit an authenticated identification
cards as basis of evaluating the signatures appearing the payrolls otherwise non-
compliance thereto would constitute issuance of Notices of Disallowance.

We recommended that in all cases, the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC)
indicate in the PO all the basic information required under COA Circular No. 96-
010 to ensure that items delivered meet specifications. We also recommended that
the DSWD submit the list of recipients of the rice, mongo, fruits and vegetables
with their signatures acknowledging receipt thereof and for the BAC to submit the
Omnibus Sworn Statement of each of the suppliers and the documentary
requirements for Mr. Llanes in compliance with Section 4(6) of P.D. No. 1445 and
Section 6 of GPPB Circular No. 01-2020 in order to ensure the validity, propriety
and legality thereof. Further, we recommended that the BAC submit the eligibility
documents of Ms. Montemayor, and explain why she still received payment as a
SAP beneficiary of ₱6,000.00. Lastly, we recommended that Management submit
a written explanation why the posting requirements under Section 9 of GPPB
Circular No. 01-2020 were not complied with.

We recommended that the Municipal see to it that 1% warranty security
for expendable and non-expendable supplies and materials are deducted from the
amount claimed to assure that manufacturing defects shall be corrected by the
supplier to protect the interest of the LGU pursuant to 62.1 of the Revised IRR of
RA 9184.

prioritize and implement the continuing projects of 20% Development Fund and require the Municipal Development Council to identify projects, programs and activities that are workable and implementable within the year to get maximum value of fund utilization considering the increasing prices of materials;
institute control mechanism to address the root cause of delay or non-implementation and facilitate timely corrective measure during the implementation cycle of the projects, programs and activities.

Recommendation:

Recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipality provide the garbage collectors and sorters with the basic personal protective equipment to avoid any health and safety hazards in the performance their duties in waste collection and segregation.

Recommendation:

Moreover, create and provide a separate appropriation relative to the administrative expenditures of the LDRRM Office to fully enhance the utilization of the said funds for pre-disaster activities.

Recommendation:
We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation that management prioritize the establishment of a permanent evacuation center intended to be fully disaster-ready at all times in accordance with the set-forth standards under DILG Memorandum Circular 122 series of 2018.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant
reconcile the cashbooks and ledgers at least quarterly in order that differences
between the two records can be adjusted right away to ensure the accuracy and
reliability of both records as well as to prevent the incurrence of cash overdrafts.

We recommended that management prioritize the filing of applications for
title of the nine untitled properties in order to secure the municipality’s ownership
thereon. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant’s and
Treasurer’s Offices maintain Real Property Ledger Cards and Property Cards,
respectively, and reconcile these records as well as records of the Municipal
Assessor regularly.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor take the appropriate steps to
register the parcels of land under the Torrens Title System, to require the
Accountant to record in the books the acquisition cost of these properties, and to
ensure that these properties are included in the annual inventory report of PPE to
be submitted to the auditor concerned not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer store the unused PVC pipes
inside the warehouse to reduce deterioration or theft. We also recommended that
the Municipal Engineer see to it that the use of these pipes be prioritized and
applied to other future projects, thus saving government resources.

We recommended that the Municipal treasurer impose the warranty either
in the form of retention money (1%) or special bank guarantee (10%) as required
under Section 62.1 of RA 9184 in order to protect the interest of the government.

We recommended that the municipal officials concerned, particularly the
Municipal Mayor and Sanggunian Bayan, amend the ordinance so that the LGU
can award the six food courts and three dry goods stalls and thus generate
additional revenues from market rentals.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor a) direct the Local Finance
Committee to study and explore possibilities of increasing local revenues in order to
minimize the municipality’s dependence on the IRA to finance its operations; b)
direct the Municipal Treasurer to verify from the records of the BIR the accuracy of
the declared gross receipts of the taxpayers in order to assess the correct amount of
tax.

We recommended that the municipality require the respective contractors to
repair the cracks and scaling in the road concreting project and to finish the works
of the day care center project otherwise their performance bonds must be forfeited
in favor of the government. We further recommended that henceforth, the
Municipal Engineer closely monitor the implementation of infrastructure projects.
Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider hiring regular
licensed engineers to augment the work force of the Municipal Engineer’s Office in
order to reduce or totally eliminate defects in the workmanship of projects.

We recommended that a) the LGU formulate and implement proper
garbage disposal procedures and maintenance of the dumpsite; b) find ways to
separate the dump site from the public cemetery; c) Secure the area with a suitable
perimeter fence so that the garbage can be contained within its boundaries and; d)
hire employees to maintain the dump site including the segregation of recyclable
and non- recyclable wastes.

Recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
Direct the Inventory Committee  to prioritize the completion of the physical inventory of all the LGU’s assets, prepare RPCPPE for each type of property, plant and equipment, reconcile the result thereof with the records of the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, and submit to COA for review and verification; and
Immediately investigate, clear and reconcile all discrepancies between physical account and accounting and property records.  If necessary, written explanations shall be required from accountable and responsible persons.

Recommendation:
We recommended that:
The management through the local Sangguniang Bayan impose higher rates on the rent of market stalls in order to sustain the operations of the market and recover the expenses which the LGU may spend on the repairs and improvements of its market building; and
The Municipal Treasurer to duly execute contracts of lease to all lessees and furnish COA with authenticated copies of these contracts.

Recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Technical Working Group should exert more efforts in securing the proof of ownership on the lots where the water supply project is located in order to avoid disruptions in the operations and maintenance of the water supply project that may be brought about by possible legal impediments arising from its lack of ownership rights.

Recommendation:
We reiterated our prior year's audit recommendation that management expedite the implementation of the LGU’s projects funded out of the 20% Development Fund in accordance with the guidelines set forth under DILG and DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 to ensure optimum utilization of the fund and to attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.

Recommendation:
We recommended that in the succeeding fiscal years:
The 1% LCPC fund allocated for the benefits of the children be expended according to the purpose for which the fund was set aside and shall include in its Municipal Development Investment Plan (MDIP) and in the Municipal Investment Program (MIP) the activities to be undertaken for the mandated 1% allocated funds for LCPC;
Prepare and submit the required reports enumerated in Memorandum Circular No. 2008-126 dated August 21, 2008 issued by DILG for easy control and monitoring of fund.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to (a) review the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF and reconcile the difference of P4.2M between the Report and the balances in the financial statements, (b) submit to COA an explanation on the noted difference and (c) furnish COA with Notes to the Financial Statements disclosing the details and amounts of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF;
Revert the Unutilized balance of CY 2011 and 2012 to general fund, as required.
Reconcile the difference in the Due to/Due from account with that of the Unutilized Balance in the Report of Utilization submitted.
Direct the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council to come up with disaster risk management activities for the LGU in order to utilize the huge accumulated balance of LDRRM fund in accordance to DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-73 which enjoined the Local Chief Executives to utilize their DRRM Fund to ensure that basic “rescue and response equipment” are procured and to operationalize the provisions of the National DRRM Plan and National Climate Change Action Plan; and
Refrain from charging the LDRRMF with expenses unrelated to the disaster risk management activities of the LGU.

Recommendation:
We recommended that  management:
Comply the required documents and make representation with the National Solid Waste Management Commission to defend their issues for the approval of the SWM Plan.
Require the Sangguniang Bayan of the LGU to pass an ordinance setting the fines and fees in the garbage collection and disposal.

Recommendations:
We recommended that the management:
Refrain from charging disbursements not allowable for the said fund;
Efficiently utilize PFPR fund accordingly for which they are intended to ensure that indigent members have better access to quality health services of the LGU.

Recommendations:
We recommended:
That the Accountant should check and verify the completeness of supporting documents attached to the vouchers/payrolls; and
That the Personnel Officer conducts a thorough review and inventory of all job orders and provide specific duties and responsibilities of job to ensure that those hired will not be performing task already being performed by the regular employees pursuant to CSC Memorandum Circular No. 1 series of 2007.

Recommendations:
We recommended that the Municipal officials  and employees strictly follow the guidelines set on the availment of the monetization of 50% or more of the accumulated of leave credits specifically Sections 22 and 23 of Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules and regulations of the Civil Service Commission.
The Designated Personnel Officer should ensure that all Applications for Leave for the commutation of leave credits are duly accomplished, computations and all data are duly present in the said form. The internal control department of the accounting section is advised not to process claims of leave credits earned without the complete supporting documents attached to the claims as set forth in the afore-cited Rules and Regulations of the Civil Service Commission.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management:
Strictly observe the provisions of RA 8291 in the deductions and remittances of GSIS premiums;
Make representation with GSIS to return to the LGU previous remittances of government share for employees no longer covered by the Act; and
Gather document and analyze available data pertaining to the unremitted premiums and adjustments of P10,939.98.  Should the premiums or loan payments be traced to the employees who made the contributions, remit the same to GSIS.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management immediately submit the remaining financial reports for CY 2017.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to review all financial records and reports in order to ascertain the validity of the account balances that should be presented in the financial statements and ensure that reports are prepared invariably in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles and Section 69 of COA Circular No. 2004-008.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exhaust all means to secure the various registries, trace the PIs that were previously transferred to the various registries, identify those that would compose the 25% requirement, prepare the necessary Journal Entry Voucher to facilitate recognition thereof in the books and, henceforth, ensure that the required rate of PI recognition is complied with every year-end until all the PIs are recorded back in the books by the end of CY 2019.
observations.managementAction: Revision of the 2016 Financial Report was done by an Accountant who made a complete revision by reposting all transactions, journalizing necessary entries and ensuring audit trails are in place.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LPRAT, through the MPDC, intensify efforts to fast-track the implementation of the BUB program for the LGU to achieve its target of reducing poverty and likewise avert any repercussions that may result from its low implementation rate by assigning technical personnel who will focus on BUB projects and by closely monitoring progress thereon. observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a) Municipal Accountant: a.1) fast-track the preparation of the BRS by ensuring that personnel assigned thereto give utmost priority to its completion in order to effect timely adjustments to the recorded balances and to pinpoint accountability for any irregular bank items or unauthorized bank transactions; a.2) establish the unreconciled amount at the beginning of CY 2016 and simultaneously commence work on two (2) fronts: 1 – workback on CY 2015 and prior years until any reconciling item is fully accounted for; and, 2 – work forward on CY 2016 up to the present without further delay, ensuring that the established reconciling item shall never increase but only decrease as work on the PY backlogs progress. b) Municipal Mayor closely monitor the regular submission of the BRS to the Auditor for verification, inexcusably for CY 2016 henceforth, and ensure immediate settlement of accountabilities for any unauthorized reconciling items so determined; and c) Management accord utmost importance to this critical and indispensable tool in order to minimize the risk of loss/misuse of depository funds and to avoid probable sanctions for gross negligence in the performance of duties.

No. 27-05, COA Circular No. 92-382, and BTr TC No. 01-2013.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Treasurer to facilitate the submission of the updated list of inventory of insurable properties with the GSIS so that the necessary insurance coverage for all the assets can be applied with the GSIS General Insurance Fund.

We recommend that management should secure Permit to Purchase from the National Telecommunication Commission and a Dealer’s Permit of the supplier issued also by the NTC to consider the transaction of purchasing Radio Handheld Transceivers, Systems and Accessories a valid and legitimate expenditure. Such supporting documents are necessary to prevent transaction from being questioned in audit.

Further, it is recommended that all signing authorities in the management should at all times exercise their respective functions and be cautious in checking the appropriateness and completeness of all supporting documents on claims against the municipality for payment as one of the requirements of sound and strong internal control system.

We recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee prepare and submit the Procurement Monitoring Report for the 1st semester of CY 2017 to GPPB website on July 14, 2017; submit the CY 2018 Annual Procurement Plan-Common-use Supplies and Equipment to DBM on November 15, 2017 and prepare the APCPI for CY 2016 during the 1st quarter of CY 2017. The same shall be done on each ensuing semesters and years thereafter.

We are recommending the management to instruct the GSO and the Accountant to comply strictly with the provisions of Sections 114 and 121 of the Manual on NGAS for the proper recording of purchases and utilization of supplies and drugs and medicines and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct affected accounts.

Require coordination between the Planning, Engineering, Treasury, Accounting, DSWD, DILG, and other concerned divisions/offices to fast track the implementation of BUB projects especially so that these projects are intended to provide benefits to the municipality’s grassroots population.
2. Require the BUB focal person to regularly monitor the status of proposed BUB projects as to funding and implementation.

Direct the General Service Officer to document the application for disposal of unserviceable property to the COA Auditor supported by the forms pursuant to the Handbook on Property and Supply Management System, and Section 79 of PD No. 1445.

Avoid the practice of procurement through reimbursement system and adopt the benefits of RA 9184 in pursuance to the declared policy of the State to promote the ideals of good governance of the LGU.

Instruct members of the Bids and Awards Committee to perform its mandated functions in accordance with the provisions of the Government Reform Act for the advantage of the LGU and paving avenue to equal opportunity among qualified and eligible bidders/suppliers in participating public bidding.

We recommend that the municipality ensure strict compliance to the provisions of Republic Act 9003 in proper segregation, collection, transport, storage, treatment and disposal of solid waste. Ensure the implementation of segregation at source of solid waste, enforcement of “no segregation, no collection policy, creation of Materials Recovery Facility (MRF) in every barangay, and the development of disposal facility in the municipality.

We recommend that management effect the transfer of its Public Infrastructure accounts to the Property, Plant and Equipment accounts of the LGU pursuant to the Philippine Public Sector Accounting Standards and COA Circular No. 2016-004 dated September 30, 2016.

We also recommend that the Local Chief Executive direct the following department heads for the compliance with the requirements of the COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015:

We recommend that in the succeeding fiscal years, the municipality shall include sufficient appropriation in the Annual Budget for Implementation of the programs of the Local Councils for the Protection of Children as required under RA 9344 and DILG Memorandum Circular 2012-120.

We recommend that the Local Chief Executive exert more effort to demand and find legal means for these collectors to produce and refund the said unremitted collections.

We also recommend that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer instruct collectors to strictly observe proper cut-off time of collections especially on Fridays and holidays and remit the same to the Municipal Treasurer intact and daily.  Such that collections received on Fridays be remitted, to prevent huge cash in their possessions.

We reiterate our previous year’s recommendation for the Municipal Assessor to expedite the cleansing and updating of the records so that the Municipal Treasurer could prepare the Certified List of the Real Property Taxpayers with the corresponding amount of RPT/SET due and collectible for each year as basis in setting up the accurate RPT/SET Receivables every beginning of the year.

We recommended the following:

Require the Budget Office to fully appropriate amount for the implementation of the MSWM Plan.

Prepare quarterly progress report and the utilization of appropriated amount to be able to evaluate the effectivity and the implementation of the plan which will serve as the basis of the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board in their review of the plan for purposes of ensuring its sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local and international developments in the field of solid waste management.

We recommend the following:

Provide at least five (5%) of the total LGU budget appropriations to support GAD activities of the LGU.

2.Strengthen the GAD Focal Point System and build capabilities of the agency on GAD thru trainings.

3.Prepare GAD Code and require the GAD Focal Point System to conduct gender analysis to be able to incorporate the programs/activities/projects (PAPs) that address gender issues in the GAD Plan and budget as well as mainstreaming in the agency operations so that the formulation, development, submission, implementation, monitoring, evaluation and accounting of results of the Municipality’s annual GPB and GAD Accomplishment Reports will be in order.

We recommend that Management furnish the Audit Team with the copies of Memorandum of Agreement (MOA)/ Trust Agreement on all funds transferred by National Government Agencies (NGAs) as provided for in Item 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 to evaluate whether the funds were utilized within the specified purpose.

We further require the submission of the requirements enumerated above to substantiate the authenticity and/or legality of the disbursement incurred by the LGU concerning the procurement of breeding animals.

We recommend compliance of AO No. 70, s. 2003 dated April 14, 2003 for the creation of Internal audit Service and its implementing guidelines under DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-4 dated March 22, 2004 for the guidelines on the organization and staffing of Internal auditing Units.
